papergraph.title: Interactions Among Tumor Subtype, PPARγ Expression, and Adipose Proliferation Shape Outcomes in Breast Cancer
papergraph.citation: Shah, A. et al. (2025) Interactions Among Tumor Subtype, PPARγ Expression, and Adipose Proliferation Shape Outcomes in Breast Cancer doi:10.1101/2025.07.08.25331111
papergraph.abstract: Breast cancer progression is influenced by tumor subtype, metabolic environment, and patient factors, including menopausal status and BMI. In this study, we utilize publicly available data to investigate the prognostic relevance of PPAR{gamma} gene expression, a key regulator of lipid metabolism, and its implications across different subgroups. We also examine the proliferation of adipose tissue in patients with various tumor subtypes and phenotypic cohorts. We analyzed RNA-seq data from 1094 primary breast cancer patients in the TCGA-BRCA cohort, stratifying patients by PPAR{gamma} expression, menopausal status, and tumor receptor subtype (ER+, HER2-, TNBC) using the UCSC Xena Browser Viewer. Kaplan-Meier analysis revealed that high PPAR{gamma} expression ([≥]6.903 FPKM) was significantly associated with both improved overall and disease-specific survival, particularly in premenopausal patients. Complementing this, we analyzed PET-CT scans from 69 breast cancer patients in the ACRIN-6888 clinical trial, focusing on SUV metrics (mean, max, peak) of a cell cycle tracer, 3-deoxy-3-[18F]-fluorothymidine (18F-FLT) in visceral and subcutaneous adipose tissue at the L3/L4 level. Postmenopausal patients had lower visceral SUVmean (0.705 vs 0.776), and patients with ER+ tumors or non-TNBC tumors showed significantly lower SUVpeak and SUVmax of adipose tissue compared to their counterparts (p < 0.05), indicating metabolic/proliferative reprogramming of adipose tissue based on tumor type. Our study provides a deeper understanding of PPAR{gamma} as a therapeutic target in breast cancer through lipid metabolism pathways. The changes in adipose tissue proliferation across cohorts demonstrate the strong potential for subtype-specific breast cancer treatment.

papergraph.scenario: breast cancer | tumor subtype stratification | adipose tissue metabolism
papergraph.contribution: High PPARγ expression is associated with improved survival, especially in premenopausal patients; adipose tissue proliferation (via 18F-FLT PET) differs significantly by tumor subtype and menopausal status, suggesting metabolic reprogramming linked to prognosis.

papergraph.title: Antibody Responses to Salmonella Typhi Antigens among Typhoid Recovered Individuals in an Endemic Region
papergraph.citation: Athavale, A. et al. (2025) Antibody Responses to Salmonella Typhi Antigens among Typhoid Recovered Individuals in an Endemic Region doi:10.1101/2025.07.04.663140
papergraph.abstract:
BackgroundTyphoid fever remains a major public health concern in endemic regions, yet the nature of antibody responses to individual Salmonella Typhi antigens and their potential role in modulating cellular immune functions are not well understood. This gap also applies to the long-term immune response after the vaccination against the disease.

ObjectivesTo evaluate the humoral and cellular immune response against the Salmonella Typhi antigens among the Typhoid recovered individuals.

MethodsELISA was used to measure antibody responses against five S. Typhi antigens in plasma samples from typhoid-recovered individuals and healthy participants. Vi polysaccharide-specific plasma antibodies were tested for their ability to activate NK cells isolated from healthy donors. Flow cytometry-based assays were performed to analyze T cell and B cell profiles in the peripheral circulation of these individuals.

ResultsPolysaccharide antigens mounted better IgA responses among typhoid-recovered individuals compared with healthy participants. Anti-S. Typhi IgM was significantly elevated in the typhoid group for all five antigens (p<0.05), while no significant differences were observed in IgG responses. NK cell activation assays showed comparable levels of activation markers of the NK cells isolated from healthy individuals and treated with the plasma of Typhoid recovered individuals. Analysis of B cell subsets revealed largely comparable distributions between the two groups; however, transitional B cells were elevated among the Typhoid recovered individuals. HlyE-specific memory B cells were detectable in both the groups. CD4 T cell subsets were similar between typhoid-recovered and healthy individuals, whereas differences were observed in CD8 T cell populations, with higher TEMRA cells among recovered individuals and relatively higher central memory CD8 T cells in healthy participants.
papergraph.conclusion: Polysaccharide antigens elicited significantly better IgA response in recovered typhoid participants, possibly reflecting mucosal immune priming from the gut. Persistent IgM antibodies after recovery suggest durable IgM responses following typhoid infection. Similar IgG levels between groups and the presence of antigen-specific memory cells in healthy individuals highlight the influence of repeated exposure in endemic settings. Elevated transitional B cells suggest the reinforcement of the B cells to the peripheral circulation during the disease which remained in the circulation of these individuals. However, elevated TEMRA population of the CD8 T cells has been observed in several other infectious diseases including this study. These findings demonstrate the complexity of humoral and cellular immune responses to S. Typhi in endemic regions.

papergraph.title: Circumcision for HIV prevention in men who have sex with men: an updated meta-analysis
papergraph.citation: Davis, S. et al. (2025) Circumcision for HIV prevention in men who have sex with men: an updated meta-analysis doi:10.1101/2025.07.02.25330772
papergraph.abstract:
BackgroundWhile circumcision reduces HIV risk for men who have sex with women, its value for men who have sex with men (MSM) remains unclear. Any protection would be for primarily-insertive MSM (PI-MSM) and could have substantial impact given high incidence. Prior meta-analyses were ambiguous due to inconsistent, highly-confounded data, leaving the total weight of evidence unclear even after a small 2024 trial in PI-MSM showed protection.

SettingNo geographic or other setting restrictions were used.

MethodsWe performed an updated literature search for data on the association of male circumcision with HIV among MSM. We conducted descriptive analyses and a random-effects meta-analysis to assess the main association among PI-MSM. To isolate the protective effect of circumcision from potential confounding factors, we also performed a confounder-adjusted meta-analysis, comparing the effect of circumcision on HIV status among primarily-insertive MSM versus among other (non-primarily-insertive) MSM in studies with stratified results.

ResultsForty-nine studies were included in the descriptive analysis, 13 in the unadjusted meta-analysis, and 10 in the confounder-adjusted meta-analysis. In the unadjusted meta-analysis, circumcision was associated with a lower risk for HIV among PI-MSM (odds ratio (OR) = 0.57, 95% CI: 0.33-0.98); publication bias was present. In the confounder-adjusted analysis, the additional effect of circumcision in the PI-MSM group - a ratio of ORs - was 0.53 (95% CI: 0.34- 0.83), indicating a lower bound on the protective effect of circumcision among PI-MSM adjusting for confounding factors. Publication bias was not present.

ConclusionsAmong PI-MSM, circumcision was protective against HIV. Findings support male circumcision as an effective HIV prevention method for PI-MSM, alongside established combination prevention methods including pre-exposure prophylaxis.
papergraph.conclusion: Among PI-MSM, circumcision was protective against HIV. Findings support male circumcision as an effective HIV prevention method for PI-MSM, alongside established combination prevention methods including pre-exposure prophylaxis.

papergraph.title: Navigating parasite antigen genetic diversity in the design of Plasmodium vivax serological exposure markers for malaria
papergraph.citation: Bareng, P. et al. (2025) Navigating parasite antigen genetic diversity in the design of Plasmodium vivax serological exposure markers for malaria doi:10.1101/2025.07.07.663616
papergraph.abstract:
BackgroundPlasmodium vivax poses a major obstacle to malaria elimination because it can lie dormant in the liver for weeks or months before reactivating and causing a relapse of infection. These dormant forms (hypnozoites) cannot be detected using standard diagnostics, but recent P. vivax exposure and by proxy, hypnozoite carriage, can be inferred using antibody-based tests (serological markers). In this study, we examined how genetic variation in P. vivax affects the utility of these antibody markers, and whether redesigned antigens could improve performance.

MethodsWe analysed global P. vivax genetic data to assess variation in leading serological markers. Based on this, we produced new antigen versions (haplotypes) that better reflect global sequence diversity, compared to the commonly used reference strain (Sal-1). Antibody responses against these new constructs were then tested using samples from well-characterised cohorts in Brazil and Thailand. Antibody levels were assessed in relation to how recently participants had a qPCR-detectable blood-stage P. vivax infection. We compared the ability of the haplotypes and reference constructs to correctly identify individuals infected within the prior 9-months.

FindingsExtensive genetic diversity was identified in two P. vivax antigens, DBPII and MSP5. Several antigens had large numbers of circulating haplotypes globally, with the percentage with similar sequence identity to the reference Sal-1 ranging from 0.4% (MSP5) to 99% (S16). Two antigens exhibited strong differences in immunogenicity by region and construct (RBP2a and DBPII). However, for most proteins (5 out of 8), these differences had little impact on the accuracy of identifying recent exposure. In cases where performance was affected (e.g. RBP2a), this could be overcome by adding multiple antigens into the classification model.

InterpretationEven highly diverse antigens can be effective serological exposure markers. Our findings highlight the importance of testing the impact of genetic diversity when designing serological tests and suggest practical strategies, such as using a mix of antigens, to ensure consistent performance across regions.

papergraph.title: Health Disparity and Geospatial Analysis among Cancer Patients in the US/Mexico Border Region of Imperial Valley
papergraph.citation: Fontelera, K. et al. (2025) Health Disparity and Geospatial Analysis among Cancer Patients in the US/Mexico Border Region of Imperial Valley doi:10.1101/2025.07.07.25331030
papergraph.abstract: PurposeDisparities in access to treatment and quality of care among cancer patients is an important issue that influences disease outcomes in minority populations. Although past research regarding the association between travel and health outcomes of cancer patients exists, studies in rural regions such as the US-Mexico border are limited. The purpose of this study is to understand the spatial patterns and travel distance association on the health outcomes of various cancers in underserved rural regions in Southern California (Imperial Valley). MethodsPatients were identified from the intake assessment data form for new patients in a community-based cancer organization (CBO) between 2006 and 2022. Analysis was conducted using ArcGIS Pro to visualize spatial patterns of cancers and to identify the relationship between travel distance and mortality. The relationship between ethnicity and cancer deaths were also investigated. Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) plots were used to visualize spatial patterns of certain groups. ResultsThere is a significant association between ethnicity and cancer deaths. The odds of death are slightly higher among Hispanics/Latinos. As hospital distance increased, the odds of mortality decreased. There were no significant associations between travel distances and any of the stages of diagnosis and death. There were three types of spatial distribution patterns of cancers respective of the overall dataset.
papergraph.conclusion: Our study found three types of spatial distribution patterns of various cancers in Imperial Valley. Cancer patient mortality did not have statistical significance associations with travel distance to treatment. However, further investigation and better tools may yield clearer results regarding its correlation.

papergraph.contribution: Identification of three distinct spatial distribution patterns of cancers in Imperial Valley and demonstration of non-significant association between travel distance and mortality despite observed ethnic disparities in cancer death.

papergraph.title: Taurine is a Natural Host Cytoadhesion Inhibitor in Asymptomatic Malaria Cases
papergraph.citation: Diffendall, G. et al. (2025) Taurine is a Natural Host Cytoadhesion Inhibitor in Asymptomatic Malaria Cases doi:10.1101/2025.07.06.663375
papergraph.abstract: The prolonged dry season in malaria-endemic regions of sub-Saharan Africa can be divided into periods of high and low transmission. The majority of symptomatic malaria cases are restricted to the short high transmission period that follows the rainy season. Shortly after, persistent asymptomatic malaria cases are more prevalent throughout the prolonged low transmission period. It is still unclear whether host metabolic alterations play a role in asymptomatic infections during seasonal malaria. In this study, we analyzed the blood plasma metabolome (n=199) of individuals in The Gambia, West Africa, capturing data from both high and low malaria transmission periods. Plasma samples from individuals (n=16) were collected monthly throughout the low transmission season, enabling a longitudinal analysis of metabolic alternations over six months. Our findings reveal that significant changes in host plasma metabolite composition are associated with seasonality and malaria pathogenicity. Notably, we observed elevated levels of taurine in asymptomatic malaria infections, especially during periods of low transmission. In vitro, this naturally occurring host molecule inhibits the cytoadhesion of malaria-infected red blood cells (iRBCs), which is key to malaria disease severity and mortality. Exogenous taurine can significantly reduce or reverse binding of iRBCs to the common adhesion receptor CD36 and the endothelial protein C receptor (EPCR), the later being associated with cerebral malaria. This study uncovers a mechanism by which elevated taurine plasma levels in asymptomatic infections could reduce cytoadhesion and lead to increased splenic clearance, thereby strengthening host resistance to symptomatic infections. In the absence of health strategies targeting dry season parasite reservoirs, our findings highlight taurine as a potential prophylactic or therapeutic agent to reduce symptomatic malaria in sub-Saharan Africa.

papergraph.title: Not Just Noise: Impaired Oscillatory Entrainment Reflects Reduced Temporal Flexibility in Autism
papergraph.citation: Beker, S. et al. (2025) Not Just Noise: Impaired Oscillatory Entrainment Reflects Reduced Temporal Flexibility in Autism doi:10.1101/2025.07.02.662876
papergraph.abstract: Rhythmic patterns in the environment enhance neural activity, perception, and action. However, natural rhythms are often imprecise, requiring flexible adaptation. In autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD), characterized by cognitive rigidity and atypical use of prior information - favoring immediate sensory input over predictive cues - entrainment to temporally variable input may be reduced at both neural and behavioral levels, though the neural mechanisms remain unclear. Here, we recorded high-density EEG and behavior in adults with ASD (n=20) and neurotypical (NT) controls (n=21) during a visual detection task with four rhythmic structures, parametrically varied from an isochronous fully regular rhythm, to a highly irregular one. Spectral analysis and temporal response function (TRF) models revealed significantly reduced modulation by temporal regularity in ASD, particularly in mildly jittered stimulation streams. Additionally, the coupling between phases of neural oscillations and behavior was diminished in ASD under the jittered conditions, suggesting reduced functional relevance of neural synchronization. Residual spectral power post-stimulation showed lower oscillatory entrainment in ASD, ruling out simple evoked-response explanations. Notably, the degree of neural modulation by temporal regularity was correlated with IQ within the ASD group, suggesting a link between temporal flexibility and individual cognitive profiles. These findings highlight impaired neural entrainment and reduced behavioral modulation by temporal structure in ASD, offering insight into inflexible responses to uncertain, volatile sensory environments.

papergraph.title: Preventing premature deaths through polygenic risk scores
papergraph.citation: Chuong, M. et al. (2025) Preventing premature deaths through polygenic risk scores doi:10.1101/2024.12.26.24319670
papergraph.abstract:
BackgroundPolygenic risk scores (PRS) have demonstrated predictive validity across a range of cohorts and diseases, but quantifying their clinical utility remains a challenge. As PRS can be derived from a single biological sample and remain stable throughout life, we explore the potential of PRS to optimize existing screening programs.

MethodsVia an integrated modelling approach, we quantified the potential clinical benefits arising from a knowledge of PRS across seven diseases with existing screening programs (abdominal aortic aneurysm, breast cancer, colorectal cancer, coronary artery disease, hypertension, prostate cancer, and type 2 diabetes). We identified individuals at high genetic risk (PRS OR>2) and very high genetic risk (PRS OR>3) and estimated the optimal screening ages for these genetically high risk individuals, based on the equivalent risk to population-level risk at recommended screening ages. We then leveraged published data on differential mortality and other outcomes, with and without screening-based interventions, to assess the potential benefits of tailoring screening age based on genetic risk. We also estimated the case enrichment ratio, which is a ratio of the percentage of cases in the high PRS risk group and in the total population.

FindingsVery high risk individuals reach the risk level associated with usual starting screening age on average 10.8 years earlier, high risk individuals 8.9 years earlier and reduced risk individuals (OR<0.5) 16.8 years later. During this time, case enrichment in the high risk group is between 1.7 and 3.0 depending on disease. Across all seven diseases, appropriate interventions following PRS-guided screening could reduce premature deaths in high risk individuals by 23.3%.

ConclusionKnowledge of genetic risk, measured using PRS, has the potential to deliver substantial public health benefits when aggregated across conditions, and could reduce premature mortality by tailoring existing screening programs.
papergraph.conclusion: Knowledge of genetic risk, measured using PRS, has the potential to deliver substantial public health benefits when aggregated across conditions, and could reduce premature mortality by tailoring existing screening programs.

papergraph.title: Network Analysis of Rare Single-Nucleotide Polymorphisms Suggests a Central Role for Type 17 Immune Responses in Trauma-induced, Genotypically Associated Hypo-Inflammation and Critical Illness
papergraph.citation: El-Dehaibi et al. (2025) Network Analysis of Rare Single-Nucleotide Polymorphisms Suggests a Central Role for Type 17 Immune Responses in Trauma-induced, Genotypically Associated Hypo-Inflammation and Critical Illness doi:10.1101/2025.01.28.25321184
papergraph.abstract: Critical illness stemming from severe traumatic injury is a leading cause of morbidity and mortality worldwide, involves the dysfunction of multiple organ systems, and is driven at least in part by dysregulated inflammation that involves type 17 immunity. We and others have demonstrated a genetic predisposition to adverse critical illness outcomes associated with single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) in novel loci distinct from those which impact type 17 immune responses yet acting in concert with those responses. We have recently developed SNPScanner, an algorithm that enables rapid scanning through a large SNP dataset and associated inflammation biomarkers and clinical data, and in the present study leveraged this algorithm in concert with existing bioinformatics tools to define networks of interaction among rare SNPs (defined operationally as being present in 5-10% of patients due to the study cohort size) assessed in critically ill trauma patients. RAR-related orphan receptor A (RORA), a transcription factor central to the differentiation of Th17 cells, was inferred as a hub gene via its SNP rs4774381; stratification of trauma patients based on homozygous genotype at this SNP pointed to distinct organ dysfunction trajectories associated with network-defined hypo-inflammation. Further, the SNP rs11919443 in the non-hub TM4SF19 gene distinguished genotype-associated systemic IL-17A trajectories, and associated hypo-inflammation with adverse outcomes. In contrast, no Th17-related hub genes were identified in a control group of SNPs associated algorithmically with adverse clinical outcomes but with genotypically indistinct systemic inflammatory responses; the main inferred hub gene in this control group was solute carrier family 2, facilitated glucose transporter member 1 (SLC2A1)/glucose transporter 1 (GLUT1). Secondary analysis showed several statistically significant differences in circulating inflammatory mediators not including IL-17A, and also associated network-based hypo-inflammation with adverse outcomes in this control group. This study thus extends our prior work aimed at defining genetic predisposition to dysregulated inflammation and pathophysiology in the context of critical illness, and points to a crucial role for type 17 immune responses.

papergraph.title: Intervention provision and engagement in Colombia's PAPSIVI: a national psychosocial support service for over half a million victims of armed conflict
papergraph.citation: Constable Fernandez, C. et al. (2025) Intervention provision and engagement in Colombia's PAPSIVI: a national psychosocial support service for over half a million victims of armed conflict doi:10.1101/2025.07.08.25331104
papergraph.abstract:
BackgroundColombias PAPSIVI program is the worlds largest psychosocial support service for victims of armed conflict providing support for over half a million civilians. However, service delivery has only previously been examined in small studies, making it difficult to understand to what extent PAPSIVI delivers interventions that are adequately targeted to individuals with the most serious exposures to the armed conflict, and to what extent attendees remain engaged with interventions.

MethodsWe investigated how different conflict exposures related to PAPSIVI intervention assignment and engagement. We linked anonymised national data from the register of victims of the Colombian armed conflict to data from N = 534,818 PAPSIVI attenders. Analysis used logistic and linear regression with cluster robust standard errors, adjusted for a range of potential confounders.

ResultsIntervention types were broadly provided in line with PAPSIVI guidelines, with victims experiencing torture, sexual violence, and forced recruitment more likely to receive individual sessions, while community-level impacts received community interventions. Female sex, ethnic minority status, and receiving state-subsidised healthcare were associated with higher intervention engagement. Those with previous mental health diagnoses had increased odds of attending individual or family sessions but lower odds of group or community sessions, consistent with recommendations for more intensive intervention for those with higher mental health needs. 29% of individual session attendees only received a single session, potentially indicating early dropout or unsuitable service provision for a proportion of attendees.

ConclusionsThis study provides insights into support provision for civilian victims of conflict indicating that psychosocial support provision can be managed effectively at very large scales.
papergraph.conclusion: This study provides insights into support provision for civilian victims of conflict indicating that psychosocial support provision can be managed effectively at very large scales.

papergraph.title: Study Protocol: Psychometric Testing of the German Vestibular Schwannoma Quality of Life Index - A Multicenter Study on Quality of Life and Patient-Centered Care in Vestibular Schwannoma
papergraph.citation: Rutenkroeger, M. et al. (2025) Study Protocol: Psychometric Testing of the German Vestibular Schwannoma Quality of Life Index - A Multicenter Study on Quality of Life and Patient-Centered Care in Vestibular Schwannoma doi:10.1101/2025.07.08.25331128
papergraph.abstract:
IntroductionVestibular schwannomas (VSs) are benign tumors of the vestibulocochlear nerve that often cause significant neurological and functional impairment, affecting patients overall quality of life (QoL). While clinical assessments have traditionally focused on hearing preservation and tumor control, patients often emphasize other critical symptoms such as dizziness, pain, cognitive difficulties and satisfaction with care. Therefore, patient-centered care that addresses the full range of patient experiences is essential. Despite its importance, patientcentered care in VS remains underexplored. This study will address this gap by psychometrically validating the German version of the Vestibular Schwannoma Quality of Life (VSQOL) Index, a newly developed QoL tool that includes key patient-centered domains. The primary objective is to validate the reliability and validity of the German VSQOL Index. The secondary aim is to assess VS patients experience of patient-centered care and its impact on their well-being.

Methods and analysisThis multicenter, cross-sectional study will involve German-speaking VS patients from several clinical centers in Germany and Switzerland as well as an online cohort. Psychometric testing of the German VSQOL will include reliability assessments (e.g. Cronbachs alpha, test-retest reliability), confirmatory factor analysis and convergent validity. In parallel, the study will assess patient-centered experiences of care using the EPAT questionnaire.

DiscussionEthical approval has been obtained and all participants will be asked to provide written informed consent. The results will be shared through scientific publications and conferences, as well as with patient groups, in order to support improvements in clinical care. By validating the German version of the VSQOL Index, this study will provide clinicians with a reliable tool for capturing patient-reported outcomes in VS, including symptoms that are often overlooked in standard assessments. Using the EPAT questionnaire alongside the VSQOL Index will provide insight into the relationship between patient experiences of care and quality of life. Together, these findings will support the delivery of more patient-centred and needs-oriented care in clinical practice and future research.

papergraph.title: Dominant negative ATP5F1A variants disrupt oxidative phosphorylation causing neurological disorders
papergraph.citation: Fielder et al. (2025) Dominant negative ATP5F1A variants disrupt oxidative phosphorylation causing neurological disorders doi:10.1101/2025.07.08.25330848
papergraph.abstract: ATP5F1A encodes the -subunit of complex V of the respiratory chain, which is responsible for mitochondrial ATP synthesis. We describe 6 probands with heterozygous de novo missense ATP5F1A variants that presented with developmental delay, intellectual disability, and movement disorders. Functional evaluation in C. elegans revealed that all variants tested were damaging to gene function via a dominant negative genetic mechanism. Biochemical and proteomics studies showed a marked reduction in complex V abundance and activity in proband-derived blood cells and fibroblasts. Mitochondrial physiology studies in fibroblasts revealed increased oxygen consumption, yet decreased mitochondrial membrane potential and ATP levels indicative of uncoupled oxidative phosphorylation as a pathophysiologic mechanism. Our findings contrast functionally and clinically with the previously reported ATP5F1A variant, p.Arg207His, suggesting a distinct pathological mechanism. This study therefore expands the phenotypic and genotypic spectrum of ATP5F1A-associated conditions and highlights how functional studies can provide understanding of the genetic, molecular, and cellular mechanisms of ATP5F1A variants of uncertain significance. With 12 heterozygous individuals now reported, ATP5F1A is the most frequent nuclear genome cause of complex V deficiency.
papergraph.scenario: neurological disorders | mitochondrial disease | complex V deficiency
papergraph.contribution: Dominant negative ATP5F1A variants disrupt oxidative phosphorylation via uncoupled mitochondrial respiration, expanding the phenotypic and genotypic spectrum of ATP5F1A-associated neurological disorders.

keynotes.title: Interactions Among Tumor Subtype, PPARγ Expression, and Adipose Proliferation Shape Outcomes in Breast Cancer

keynote.title: Interactions Among Tumor Subtype, PPARγ Expression, and Adipose Proliferation Shape Outcomes in Breast Cancer
keynote.core_problem: Understanding how tumor subtype, PPARγ expression, and adipose tissue proliferation interact to influence breast cancer outcomes, particularly survival and metabolic reprogramming.
contributions: Identified prognostic relevance of PPARγ expression across menopausal status and receptor subtypes (ER+, HER2-, TNBC)
Demonstrated subtype- and menopausal status–associated differences in adipose tissue proliferation using 18F-FLT PET-CT metrics
Linked PPARγ expression to improved overall and disease-specific survival, especially in premenopausal patients
Revealed metabolic/proliferative reprogramming of adipose tissue based on tumor biology
methods: Kaplan-Meier survival analysis stratified by PPARγ expression (≥6.903 FPKM), menopausal status, and receptor subtype
RNA-seq data analysis from TCGA-BRCA (n=1094)
Quantitative PET-CT analysis of 18F-FLT uptake (SUVmean, SUVmax, SUVpeak) in visceral and subcutaneous adipose tissue at L3/L4 level
Analysis of ACRIN-6888 clinical trial imaging data (n=69)
experiments_or_evidence: TCGA-BRCA RNA-seq cohort: high PPARγ associated with improved overall and disease-specific survival (premenopausal subgroup highlighted)
ACRIN-6888 PET-CT cohort: lower visceral SUVmean in postmenopausal patients (0.705 vs 0.776); lower SUVpeak/SUVmax in ER+ and non-TNBC tumors vs counterparts (p < 0.05)

gap_signals.text: Limited sample size for PET-CT analysis (n=69), potentially underpowered for subgroup analyses beyond ER+/TNBC and menopausal status.
Reliance on retrospective public datasets (TCGA, ACRIN-6888) without prospective validation or mechanistic experimentation.

datasets_or_materials: TCGA-BRCA RNA-seq data (n=1094)
ACRIN-6888 clinical trial PET-CT data (n=69)
UCSC Xena Browser Viewer for data access and stratification

important_claims.claim: High PPARγ expression (≥6.903 FPKM) is significantly associated with improved overall and disease-specific survival, particularly in premenopausal patients.
important_claims.evidence: Kaplan-Meier analysis of TCGA-BRCA cohort.

important_claims.claim: Postmenopausal patients exhibit lower visceral SUVmean than premenopausal patients; ER+ and non-TNBC tumors associate with lower adipose SUVpeak and SUVmax.
important_claims.evidence: Quantitative 18F-FLT PET-CT analysis in ACRIN-6888 cohort (p < 0.05).

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides reusable analytical framework linking transcriptomic biomarkers (PPARγ) with functional imaging metrics (18F-FLT SUV) across clinical strata; enables hypothesis generation for metabolic targeting in subtype- and menopause-stratified trials.

keynotes.title: Antibody Responses to Salmonella Typhi Antigens among Typhoid Recovered Individuals in an Endemic Region

keynote.title: Antibody Responses to Salmonella Typhi Antigens among Typhoid Recovered Individuals in an Endemic Region
keynote.core_problem: Poor understanding of antigen-specific antibody responses to Salmonella Typhi and their modulation of cellular immunity in typhoid-recovered individuals in endemic regions, including long-term post-infection and post-vaccination immunity.
contributions: Characterized humoral (IgA, IgM, IgG) responses against five S. Typhi antigens in typhoid-recovered vs. healthy individuals.
Assessed functional impact of Vi polysaccharide-specific antibodies on NK cell activation.
Defined peripheral B and T cell subset distributions (including transitional B cells, HlyE-specific memory B cells, CD4/CD8 subsets, TEMRA vs. central memory CD8 T cells) in recovered individuals.
methods: ELISA for quantifying antigen-specific IgA, IgM, and IgG against five S. Typhi antigens.
NK cell activation assays using plasma from recovered individuals and NK cells from healthy donors.
Flow cytometry-based immunophenotyping of peripheral B cell subsets (including transitional and HlyE-specific memory B cells) and T cell subsets (CD4, CD8, TEMRA, central memory).
experiments_or_evidence: Significantly elevated anti-S. Typhi IgM in recovered individuals for all five antigens (p<0.05).
Enhanced IgA responses to polysaccharide antigens in recovered individuals vs. healthy controls.
No significant differences in IgG responses between groups.
Comparable NK cell activation markers upon treatment with Vi-specific plasma.
Elevated transitional B cells in recovered individuals; detectable HlyE-specific memory B cells in both groups.
Similar CD4 T cell subsets; increased CD8 TEMRA cells in recovered individuals, higher central memory CD8 T cells in healthy participants.
assumptions: Plasma-mediated NK cell activation reflects in vivo functional relevance of anti-Vi antibodies.
Peripheral blood immune profiling adequately represents systemic immune status post-typhoid.
Presence of antigen-specific memory B cells (e.g., HlyE) implies functional relevance despite lack of differential abundance.
limitations: Lack of longitudinal sampling limits inference about durability or evolution of responses.
Use of plasma from recovered individuals + NK cells from healthy donors may not reflect autologous interactions.
No data on vaccination history or prior exposure stratification within healthy controls.
Functional assays limited to NK activation; no assessment of macrophage/neutrophil engagement or T cell functionality (e.g., cytokine production, cytotoxicity).
Five antigens tested, but full antigenic repertoire of S. Typhi not covered.

gap_signals.text: Five antigens tested, but full antigenic repertoire of S. Typhi not covered.
Functional assays limited to NK activation; no assessment of macrophage/neutrophil engagement or T cell functionality (e.g., cytokine production, cytotoxicity).
Lack of longitudinal sampling limits inference about durability or evolution of responses.
No data on vaccination history or prior exposure stratification within healthy controls.
Use of plasma from recovered individuals + NK cells from healthy donors may not reflect autologous interactions.
Nature of antibody responses to individual S. Typhi antigens and their role in modulating cellular immune functions is not well understood.
The gap also applies to the long-term immune response after vaccination against the disease.

datasets_or_materials: Plasma samples from typhoid-recovered individuals and healthy participants from an endemic region.
NK cells isolated from healthy donors.
Peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) for flow cytometry (implied by B/T cell profiling).

important_claims.claim: Polysaccharide antigens mounted better IgA responses among typhoid-recovered individuals compared with healthy participants.
important_claims.evidence: Reported result; interpreted as possibly reflecting mucosal immune priming from the gut.

important_claims.claim: Anti-S. Typhi IgM was significantly elevated in the typhoid group for all five antigens (p<0.05).
important_claims.evidence: Statistical result explicitly stated.

important_claims.claim: Transitional B cells were elevated among typhoid-recovered individuals.
important_claims.evidence: Reported finding from flow cytometry analysis.

important_claims.claim: CD8 TEMRA cells were higher in typhoid-recovered individuals, whereas healthy participants had relatively higher central memory CD8 T cells.
important_claims.evidence: Reported comparative finding from flow cytometry analysis.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides benchmark antigen-specific serological and immune phenotyping data from a typhoid-endemic setting; identifies candidate correlates (e.g., IgM persistence, transitional B cells, CD8 TEMRA) for further mechanistic or vaccine studies; highlights need for functional validation beyond surface phenotyping.

keynotes.title: Circumcision for HIV prevention in men who have sex with men: an updated meta-analysis

keynote.title: Circumcision for HIV prevention in men who have sex with men: an updated meta-analysis
keynote.core_problem: Uncertainty about whether male circumcision reduces HIV risk among men who have sex with men (MSM), particularly primarily-insertive MSM (PI-MSM), due to inconsistent and highly confounded prior evidence.
contributions: Updated meta-analysis incorporating new evidence including a 2024 trial in PI-MSM
First confounder-adjusted meta-analysis comparing circumcision's effect specifically within PI-MSM versus other MSM using stratified study data
Quantification of lower bound on protective effect of circumcision among PI-MSM after adjusting for confounding (ratio of ORs = 0.53)
methods: Updated literature search for studies on male circumcision and HIV among MSM
Descriptive analysis across 49 studies
Random-effects meta-analysis (unadjusted) across 13 studies focusing on PI-MSM
Confounder-adjusted meta-analysis across 10 studies with stratified results, estimating ratio of odds ratios (PI-MSM OR / non-PI-MSM OR)
experiments_or_evidence: Unadjusted meta-analysis: OR = 0.57 (95% CI: 0.33–0.98) for circumcision and HIV among PI-MSM
Confounder-adjusted analysis: ratio of ORs = 0.53 (95% CI: 0.34–0.83), indicating circumcision’s additional protective effect in PI-MSM after accounting for confounding
Assessment of publication bias: present in unadjusted analysis; absent in confounder-adjusted analysis
assumptions: Circumcision’s biological mechanism of protection (e.g., reduced mucosal exposure) applies similarly in PI-MSM as in heterosexual transmission contexts
Stratified data reported in included studies reliably distinguish PI-MSM from other MSM
Residual confounding is adequately addressed by the ratio-of-ORs approach in studies with stratified reporting
limitations: Publication bias detected in unadjusted meta-analysis
Limited number of studies contributing to confounder-adjusted analysis (n = 10)
Heterogeneity in definitions and measurement of insertive role across studies
Inability to adjust for unmeasured or poorly reported confounders (e.g., condom use, partner concurrency, PrEP uptake) in most studies

gap_signals.text: Heterogeneity in definitions and measurement of insertive role across studies
Inability to adjust for unmeasured or poorly reported confounders (e.g., condom use, partner concurrency, PrEP uptake) in most studies
Limited number of studies contributing to confounder-adjusted analysis (n = 10)
Publication bias detected in unadjusted meta-analysis
Prior meta-analyses were ambiguous due to inconsistent, highly-confounded data.
Unclear total weight of evidence even after a small 2024 trial in PI-MSM showed protection.
No prior confounder-adjusted meta-analysis comparing circumcision’s effect specifically within PI-MSM versus other MSM using stratified results.

important_claims.claim: Among primarily-insertive MSM, circumcision was protective against HIV.
important_claims.evidence: Confounder-adjusted ratio of ORs = 0.53 (95% CI: 0.34–0.83); unadjusted OR = 0.57 (95% CI: 0.33–0.98).

important_claims.claim: The protective effect of circumcision among PI-MSM persists after adjusting for confounding factors.
important_claims.evidence: Confounder-adjusted analysis showed no publication bias and a robust ratio of ORs < 1 with tight confidence interval excluding 1.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a methodological template for confounder-adjusted meta-analysis using stratified subgroup comparisons; highlights need for standardized behavioral role classification and granular exposure reporting in future MSM HIV studies.

keynotes.title: Navigating parasite antigen genetic diversity in the design of Plasmodium vivax serological exposure markers for malaria

keynote.title: Navigating parasite antigen genetic diversity in the design of Plasmodium vivax serological exposure markers for malaria
keynote.core_problem: Genetic diversity in Plasmodium vivax antigens undermines the reliability of serological exposure markers across geographic regions, complicating hypnozoite carriage inference and malaria elimination efforts.
contributions: Quantified global genetic diversity across 8 P. vivax serological marker antigens (e.g., DBPII, MSP5, RBP2a, S16)
Designed and produced antigen haplotypes reflecting global sequence diversity, moving beyond the Sal-1 reference strain
Empirically validated new haplotype constructs using field samples from Brazil and Thailand cohorts
Demonstrated that antigen diversity impacts immunogenicity regionally but not always diagnostic accuracy—and that multi-antigen models mitigate performance loss
methods: Analysis of global P. vivax genetic sequence data to assess antigen variation
Design and production of antigen haplotypes representing prevalent global variants
Serological testing of antibody responses against haplotype vs. Sal-1 reference constructs
Correlation of antibody levels with timing of recent qPCR-detectable blood-stage infection (within prior 9 months)
Comparison of classification accuracy (recent infection identification) between haplotype and reference antigens
experiments_or_evidence: Antibody response testing in well-characterised cohorts from Brazil and Thailand
Assessment of antibody levels relative to time since qPCR-confirmed P. vivax infection
Evaluation of classification accuracy for recent infection (≤9 months) using haplotype vs. Sal-1 antigens
Regional comparison of immunogenicity (e.g., RBP2a and DBPII showed strong regional differences)
assumptions: Antibody levels correlate with recent P. vivax blood-stage infection and thus serve as proxies for hypnozoite carriage
Genetic diversity in antigens directly affects antibody recognition and assay performance
Haplotypes derived from global sequence data adequately represent immunologically relevant variation
limitations: Cohorts limited to Brazil and Thailand — geographic generalisability untested elsewhere
Only 8 antigens examined; broader antigen panel not assessed
No direct validation of hypnozoite carriage (only inferred via recent blood-stage infection)
Functional impact of specific mutations on antibody binding not experimentally characterised

gap_signals.text: Cohorts limited to Brazil and Thailand — geographic generalisability untested elsewhere
Functional impact of specific mutations on antibody binding not experimentally characterised
No direct validation of hypnozoite carriage (only inferred via recent blood-stage infection)
Only 8 antigens examined; broader antigen panel not assessed
Lack of systematic evaluation of antigen diversity impact on serological assay performance across endemic regions
Reliance on single-reference (Sal-1) antigens despite known global diversity
Limited field validation of serological markers outside few sites; no direct hypnozoite detection correlation

datasets_or_materials: Global P. vivax genetic sequence data
Serum samples from well-characterised cohorts in Brazil and Thailand
qPCR-confirmed P. vivax infection timing metadata

important_claims.claim: Extensive genetic diversity exists in key P. vivax antigens (e.g., DBPII and MSP5), with only 0.4% of global MSP5 haplotypes sharing high identity with Sal-1
important_claims.evidence: Analysis of global P. vivax genetic data showed variation in sequence identity to Sal-1 ranging from 0.4% (MSP5) to 99% (S16)

important_claims.claim: Regional differences in immunogenicity (e.g., for RBP2a and DBPII) affect serological assay performance
important_claims.evidence: Two antigens exhibited strong regional and construct-specific differences in antibody response

important_claims.claim: Multi-antigen models compensate for diversity-driven performance loss in individual antigens
important_claims.evidence: Where performance was affected (e.g., RBP2a), adding multiple antigens into the classification model overcame the deficit

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a template for diversity-aware serological marker development: antigen haplotype design, cohort-based cross-regional validation, and multi-antigen modelling strategies applicable to other genetically diverse pathogens.

keynotes.title: Health Disparity and Geospatial Analysis among Cancer Patients in the US/Mexico Border Region of Imperial Valley

keynote.title: Health Disparity and Geospatial Analysis among Cancer Patients in the US/Mexico Border Region of Imperial Valley
keynote.core_problem: Understanding spatial patterns and the association between travel distance to care and health outcomes (e.g., mortality) for cancer patients in an underserved, rural, minority-dominant US-Mexico border region (Imperial Valley).
contributions: Identified three distinct spatial distribution patterns of cancers in Imperial Valley.
Reported a non-significant association between travel distance to treatment and cancer mortality.
Documented a significant association between Hispanic/Latino ethnicity and higher odds of cancer death.
methods: Retrospective analysis of intake assessment data from a community-based cancer organization (CBO) (2006–2022).
Geospatial visualization and analysis using ArcGIS Pro.
Kernel Density Estimation (KDE) plots to visualize spatial patterns by demographic/cancer group.
Statistical modeling to assess associations between travel distance, ethnicity, diagnosis stage, and mortality.
experiments_or_evidence: Odds ratio analysis showing slightly higher odds of death among Hispanics/Latinos.
Statistical testing showing no significant association between travel distance and mortality, diagnosis stage, or death.
KDE-based mapping revealing three spatial distribution patterns across cancer types.
assumptions: Intake assessment data accurately reflect patient residence, diagnosis, and outcome status.
Travel distance (likely straight-line or network-based) is a valid proxy for access to care.
Mortality outcomes are reliably captured and reported in the CBO dataset.
limitations: Limited generalizability due to single-community, community-based organization data source.
Retrospective design with potential missing or inconsistent longitudinal follow-up (e.g., vital status, treatment details).
Lack of granular clinical variables (e.g., cancer subtype, treatment modality, comorbidities).
Absence of validated travel time or multimodal transportation metrics (e.g., public transit access, insurance coverage).

gap_signals.text: Limited generalizability due to single-community, community-based organization data source.
Absence of validated travel time or multimodal transportation metrics (e.g., public transit access, insurance coverage).
Lack of granular clinical variables (e.g., cancer subtype, treatment modality, comorbidities).
Retrospective design with potential missing or inconsistent longitudinal follow-up (e.g., vital status, treatment details).
Studies in rural regions such as the US-Mexico border are limited.
Further investigation and better tools may yield clearer results regarding [travel distance–mortality] correlation.

datasets_or_materials: Intake assessment data forms from a community-based cancer organization (CBO) in Imperial Valley (2006–2022).

important_claims.claim: The odds of death are slightly higher among Hispanics/Latinos.
important_claims.evidence: Statistical analysis reported in Results section.

important_claims.claim: As hospital distance increased, the odds of mortality decreased.
important_claims.evidence: Explicitly stated in Results section.

important_claims.claim: There were no significant associations between travel distances and any of the stages of diagnosis and death.
important_claims.evidence: Explicitly stated in Results section.

important_claims.claim: There are three types of spatial distribution patterns of cancers in Imperial Valley.
important_claims.evidence: Explicitly stated in Results section; visualized via KDE plots.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a geospatial baseline and methodological template (KDE + ArcGIS Pro + logistic modeling) for studying cancer disparities in other rural, cross-border, or minority-serving settings; highlights need for richer mobility and clinical covariates in access-to-care studies.

keynotes.title: Taurine is a Natural Host Cytoadhesion Inhibitor in Asymptomatic Malaria Cases

keynote.title: Taurine is a Natural Host Cytoadhesion Inhibitor in Asymptomatic Malaria Cases
keynote.core_problem: It is unclear whether host metabolic alterations contribute to asymptomatic malaria persistence during low transmission seasons in sub-Saharan Africa.
contributions: Identified elevated taurine levels in plasma of individuals with asymptomatic malaria, particularly during low transmission periods.
Demonstrated that taurine inhibits cytoadhesion of Plasmodium falciparum-infected red blood cells (iRBCs) to CD36 and EPCR in vitro.
Proposed a mechanistic link between taurine-mediated reduction of cytoadhesion and increased splenic clearance, contributing to asymptomatic infection maintenance.
methods: Plasma metabolome profiling (n=199) across high and low malaria transmission periods in The Gambia.
Longitudinal plasma sampling (n=16 individuals, monthly over six months during low transmission season).
In vitro cytoadhesion assays measuring iRBC binding to CD36 and endothelial protein C receptor (EPCR).
Exogenous taurine treatment in adhesion assays to assess functional inhibition.
experiments_or_evidence: Metabolomic analysis revealing season- and pathogenicity-associated plasma metabolite changes.
Quantification of elevated taurine in asymptomatic vs. symptomatic cases during low transmission.
In vitro reduction/reversal of iRBC binding to CD36 and EPCR upon taurine exposure.
assumptions: Cytoadhesion inhibition by taurine observed in vitro is physiologically relevant in vivo.
Elevated plasma taurine contributes causally—not just correlatively—to asymptomatic infection maintenance.
Splenic clearance enhancement is the primary downstream consequence of reduced cytoadhesion in this context.

gap_signals.text: Findings are based on a single geographic cohort (The Gambia); generalizability to other malaria-endemic regions or Plasmodium species is unaddressed.
Mechanistic evidence linking taurine elevation to clinical outcomes (e.g., duration of asymptomatic carriage, progression to symptoms) is not provided.
No in vivo or clinical interventional data testing taurine supplementation for prophylaxis or therapy.

datasets_or_materials: Human plasma samples (n=199 cross-sectional; n=16 longitudinal) from The Gambia, West Africa.
Plasma metabolomics dataset (unspecified platform, e.g., LC-MS or NMR implied but not named).

important_claims.claim: Elevated taurine in plasma is associated with asymptomatic malaria during low transmission seasons.
important_claims.evidence: Metabolomic profiling of 199 plasma samples across transmission seasons; longitudinal sampling (n=16) confirmed sustained elevation during low transmission.

important_claims.claim: Taurine inhibits iRBC cytoadhesion to CD36 and EPCR in vitro.
important_claims.evidence: Exogenous taurine significantly reduced or reversed iRBC binding in adhesion assays targeting CD36 and EPCR.

important_claims.claim: Taurine-mediated cytoadhesion inhibition may promote splenic clearance and sustain asymptomatic infection.
important_claims.evidence: Hypothesized mechanism based on established roles of cytoadhesion in sequestration and splenic filtration; no direct experimental validation of splenic clearance provided.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a testable host-derived metabolic biomarker and intervention candidate (taurine) for asymptomatic reservoir targeting; metabolomic dataset enables replication and integration with host-genetic or parasite-genomic studies.

keynotes.title: Not Just Noise: Impaired Oscillatory Entrainment Reflects Reduced Temporal Flexibility in Autism

keynote.title: Not Just Noise: Impaired Oscillatory Entrainment Reflects Reduced Temporal Flexibility in Autism
keynote.core_problem: Impaired neural and behavioral adaptation to temporally variable (non-isochronous) rhythmic input in autism spectrum disorder (ASD), linked to cognitive rigidity and atypical predictive processing.
contributions: Demonstrates reduced neural modulation by temporal regularity in ASD across parametrically varied rhythmic structures.
Shows diminished phase–behavior coupling under jittered (mildly irregular) conditions in ASD, indicating reduced functional relevance of neural synchronization.
Rules out evoked-response explanations via residual spectral power analysis post-stimulation.
Reports a positive correlation between neural modulation by temporal regularity and IQ within the ASD group.
methods: High-density EEG recording during a visual detection task.
Parametric manipulation of rhythmic structure: four levels from isochronous to highly irregular (including mildly jittered streams).
Spectral analysis (e.g., time-frequency power, residual oscillatory power).
Temporal Response Function (TRF) modeling.
Phase–behavior coupling analysis (e.g., phase-amplitude or phase–response alignment).
experiments_or_evidence: Group comparison (ASD n=20 vs. NT n=21) of neural modulation by temporal regularity.
Reduced modulation in ASD, especially in mildly jittered conditions.
Diminished neural-behavioral phase coupling in ASD under jittered stimulation.
Lower residual spectral power post-stimulation in ASD, indicating reduced sustained entrainment.
Within-ASD correlation between neural modulation strength and IQ.
assumptions: Neural entrainment to rhythmic input reflects temporal flexibility.
Phase–behavior coupling is a valid proxy for functional relevance of oscillatory synchronization.
Residual spectral power post-stimulus reflects endogenous entrainment rather than evoked transients.
limitations: Adult-only sample (no developmental or pediatric data).
No causal manipulation (e.g., no entrainment-based intervention or perturbation).
Limited generalizability beyond visual domain and detection task.
Abstract truncation prevents confirmation of full methodological or analytic details (e.g., exact jitter parameters, TRF implementation, statistical thresholds).

gap_signals.text: Abstract truncation prevents confirmation of full methodological or analytic details (e.g., exact jitter parameters, TRF implementation, statistical thresholds).
Adult-only sample (no developmental or pediatric data).
Limited generalizability beyond visual domain and detection task.
No causal manipulation (e.g., no entrainment-based intervention or perturbation).
Neural mechanisms underlying impaired entrainment to temporally variable input in ASD remain unclear.
Lack of causal or perturbative tests (e.g., rhythmic tACS) to probe mechanism.
Findings based on adults only; developmental trajectory of temporal flexibility deficits unexamined.

datasets_or_materials: High-density EEG recordings from 20 adults with ASD and 21 neurotypical controls.
Behavioral responses (visual detection task) synchronized with parametrically jittered rhythmic stimuli.

important_claims.claim: Neural modulation by temporal regularity is significantly reduced in ASD, especially under mild jitter.
important_claims.evidence: Spectral analysis and TRF models revealed significantly reduced modulation in ASD compared to NT controls, most pronounced in mildly jittered stimulation streams.

important_claims.claim: Phase–behavior coupling is diminished in ASD under jittered conditions.
important_claims.evidence: Coupling between neural oscillation phases and behavioral responses was weaker in ASD specifically during jittered stimulation.

important_claims.claim: Reduced entrainment is not explained by attenuated evoked responses.
important_claims.evidence: Residual spectral power post-stimulation was lower in ASD, indicating impaired sustained oscillatory entrainment independent of transient evoked activity.

important_claims.claim: Neural modulation by temporal regularity correlates with IQ in ASD.
important_claims.evidence: Within-group correlation observed between degree of neural modulation and IQ score in the ASD cohort.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a validated EEG/behavioral paradigm for quantifying temporal flexibility; offers testable biomarkers (e.g., jitter-sensitive TRF slope, residual alpha/beta power) for cross-study replication and clinical stratification; highlights parametric rhythm jitter as a sensitive probe for predictive timing deficits.

keynotes.title: Preventing premature deaths through polygenic risk scores

keynote.title: Preventing premature deaths through polygenic risk scores
keynote.core_problem: Quantifying the clinical utility of polygenic risk scores (PRS) for optimizing existing disease screening programs to prevent premature deaths.
contributions: Quantified the potential clinical benefits of PRS-guided screening across seven diseases with established screening programs.
Estimated optimal, genetically informed screening ages based on equivalent population-level risk thresholds.
Reported case enrichment ratios for high-PRS groups across diseases.
Projected a 23.3% reduction in premature deaths among high-risk individuals via PRS-tailored screening.
methods: Integrated modelling approach linking PRS-derived risk (OR > 2 for high risk; OR > 3 for very high risk; OR < 0.5 for reduced risk) to age-specific disease risk.
Calculated optimal screening ages by equating genetic-risk trajectories to population-level risk at standard screening ages.
Leveraged published data on mortality and outcomes with/without screening-based interventions to estimate benefits.
Computed case enrichment ratio as (percentage of cases in high-PRS group) / (percentage of cases in total population).
experiments_or_evidence: Modelled time shifts in risk-equivalent screening age: very high risk individuals reach standard screening risk level 10.8 years earlier; high risk, 8.9 years earlier; reduced risk, 16.8 years later.
Reported case enrichment between 1.7–3.0 across the seven diseases.
Projected 23.3% reduction in premature deaths among high-risk individuals across all seven diseases under PRS-guided screening.
assumptions: PRS OR thresholds (e.g., OR > 2, OR > 3) meaningfully stratify clinically relevant risk.
Age-specific risk trajectories derived from PRS are stable and generalizable across populations.
Screening interventions applied at genetically optimized ages yield proportional mortality reductions as observed in existing trial or observational evidence.
No major confounding by non-genetic factors (e.g., environment, access) in projected benefit estimates.
limitations: Reliance on published data rather than primary empirical validation of PRS-guided screening outcomes.
Lack of reported validation in diverse or real-world healthcare settings.
No discussion of PRS portability across ancestries or calibration in underrepresented populations.
No accounting for potential harms (e.g., overdiagnosis, anxiety, resource burden) of earlier or more intensive screening.

gap_signals.text: No accounting for potential harms (e.g., overdiagnosis, anxiety, resource burden) of earlier or more intensive screening.
Lack of reported validation in diverse or real-world healthcare settings.
No discussion of PRS portability across ancestries or calibration in underrepresented populations.
Reliance on published data rather than primary empirical validation of PRS-guided screening outcomes.
No discussion of feasibility, cost, ethics, or integration pathways into clinical workflows.
No primary experimental validation — benefits are modelled, not empirically measured.
No mention of ancestry diversity or PRS performance outside European-ancestry cohorts.

important_claims.claim: Very high risk individuals reach the risk level associated with usual starting screening age on average 10.8 years earlier.
important_claims.evidence: Integrated modelling using PRS OR > 3 and equivalence to population-level risk at standard screening ages.

important_claims.claim: Case enrichment in the high risk group is between 1.7 and 3.0 depending on disease.
important_claims.evidence: Calculated case enrichment ratio across seven diseases using PRS-stratified prevalence data.

important_claims.claim: Appropriate interventions following PRS-guided screening could reduce premature deaths in high risk individuals by 23.3%.
important_claims.evidence: Leveraged published mortality and intervention outcome data within an integrated modelling framework.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a generalizable modelling framework for estimating PRS-driven screening age shifts and mortality impact—reusable for other diseases or risk scores, pending calibration and validation.

keynotes.title: Network Analysis of Rare Single-Nucleotide Polymorphisms Suggests a Central Role for Type 17 Immune Responses in Trauma-induced, Genotypically Associated Hypo-Inflammation and Critical Illness

keynote.title: Network Analysis of Rare Single-Nucleotide Polymorphisms Suggests a Central Role for Type 17 Immune Responses in Trauma-induced, Genotypically Associated Hypo-Inflammation and Critical Illness
keynote.core_problem: Genetic predisposition to dysregulated inflammation—specifically trauma-induced hypo-inflammation—and adverse critical illness outcomes, despite known involvement of type 17 immunity.
contributions: Identification of RORA (via rs4774381) as a hub gene linking rare SNPs to Th17-driven hypo-inflammation and organ dysfunction trajectories in trauma patients.
Demonstration that SNP rs11919443 in TM4SF19 associates with systemic IL-17A trajectories and hypo-inflammation linked to adverse outcomes.
Contrastive network analysis showing absence of Th17-related hub genes in a control SNP group associated with adverse outcomes but lacking genotypically distinct inflammatory phenotypes.
Extension of prior work using SNPScanner to integrate rare SNPs, inflammation biomarkers, and clinical outcomes in critical illness.
methods: SNPScanner algorithm for rapid scanning of large SNP datasets integrated with inflammation biomarkers and clinical data.
Network analysis of rare SNPs (5–10% population frequency) in critically ill trauma patients.
Hub gene inference via network topology (e.g., centrality metrics).
Genotype stratification (e.g., homozygous vs. heterozygous/variant) to assess organ dysfunction trajectories and cytokine dynamics.
Secondary statistical analysis of circulating inflammatory mediators (excluding IL-17A) in control SNP group.
experiments_or_evidence: Stratification by RORA rs4774381 homozygosity revealed distinct organ dysfunction trajectories linked to network-defined hypo-inflammation.
SNP rs11919443 in TM4SF19 distinguished genotype-associated systemic IL-17A trajectories and hypo-inflammation correlated with adverse outcomes.
Control SNP group showed no Th17-related hub genes; primary hub was SLC2A1/GLUT1.
Secondary analysis identified statistically significant differences in non-IL-17A inflammatory mediators associated with network-based hypo-inflammation and adverse outcomes in control group.
assumptions: Rare SNPs (5–10% frequency) are operationally meaningful proxies for functional genetic influence in this cohort.
Hub gene inference from SNP interaction networks reflects biologically relevant regulatory roles (e.g., RORA in Th17 differentiation).
Genotype–phenotype associations observed are causal or mechanistically proximal, not merely correlative.

gap_signals.text: Absence of Th17-related hub genes in control SNP group—despite association with adverse outcomes—suggests type 17 immunity is specifically implicated only when hypo-inflammation is genotypically structured, not in all adverse-outcome genetics.
Operational definition of 'rare' as 5–10% frequency is cohort-size-dependent and may not generalize to broader populations or rarer variants (<1%).

datasets_or_materials: Genotype data (SNPs) from critically ill trauma patients.
Inflammation biomarker data (including IL-17A and other circulating mediators).
Clinical outcome data (organ dysfunction trajectories, mortality, morbidity).
Control SNP group defined algorithmically for adverse outcomes without genotypically distinct inflammatory responses.
code_or_implementation: SNPScanner algorithm (developed by authors, no public repository or implementation details provided).

important_claims.claim: RORA (via rs4774381) is a hub gene whose homozygous genotype stratifies trauma patients into distinct organ dysfunction trajectories linked to network-defined hypo-inflammation.
important_claims.evidence: Genotype stratification analysis showing differential organ dysfunction trajectories correlated with network-inferred hypo-inflammation.

important_claims.claim: SNP rs11919443 in TM4SF19 distinguishes systemic IL-17A trajectories and associates hypo-inflammation with adverse outcomes.
important_claims.evidence: Association between rs11919443 genotype, IL-17A dynamics, and adverse clinical outcomes.

important_claims.claim: Th17-related hub genes are absent in a control SNP group associated with adverse outcomes but lacking genotypically distinct inflammatory phenotypes.
important_claims.evidence: Network analysis identifying SLC2A1/GLUT1—not Th17 genes—as the main hub in the control group.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a network-based framework (via SNPScanner + bioinformatics integration) to link rare SNPs to immune endotypes in critical illness; enables hypothesis generation for causal testing of RORA/TM4SF19 in Th17 modulation and hypo-inflammatory pathophysiology.

keynotes.title: Intervention provision and engagement in Colombia's PAPSIVI: a national psychosocial support service for over half a million victims of armed conflict

keynote.title: Intervention provision and engagement in Colombia's PAPSIVI: a national psychosocial support service for over half a million victims of armed conflict
keynote.core_problem: Limited understanding of how well Colombia's national psychosocial support program (PAPSIVI) targets interventions to victims with most severe conflict exposures, and how well it sustains engagement across diverse subpopulations.
contributions: First large-scale empirical analysis linking national victim registry data to PAPSIVI service delivery data (N = 534,818 attenders).
Evidence on alignment between conflict exposure profiles and intervention type assignment per program guidelines.
Identification of demographic and clinical factors associated with differential engagement across intervention modalities (individual, family, group, community).
Quantification of early attrition: 29% of individual session attendees received only one session.
methods: Linkage of anonymised national victim register data with PAPSIVI attendance records.
Logistic and linear regression with cluster robust standard errors.
Adjustment for potential confounders (not enumerated in text).
experiments_or_evidence: Regression analyses showing victims exposed to torture, sexual violence, or forced recruitment were more likely to receive individual sessions.
Regression analyses showing victims experiencing community-level conflict impacts were more likely to receive community interventions.
Association of female sex, ethnic minority status, and state-subsidised healthcare receipt with higher intervention engagement.
Association of prior mental health diagnosis with higher odds of attending individual/family sessions but lower odds of group/community sessions.
Descriptive finding that 29% of individual session attendees attended only one session.
limitations: No information on causal mechanisms or intervention fidelity.
No details on confounder selection or measurement.
No qualitative or process data explaining reasons for low engagement or single-session attendance.
No longitudinal tracking of outcomes or mental health improvements.

gap_signals.text: No details on confounder selection or measurement.
No information on causal mechanisms or intervention fidelity.
No longitudinal tracking of outcomes or mental health improvements.
No qualitative or process data explaining reasons for low engagement or single-session attendance.
Service delivery has only previously been examined in small studies.
Limited evidence on how sociodemographic and clinical subgroups experience service access and retention.
Difficulty understanding targeting and engagement at national scale.

datasets_or_materials: Anonymised national register of victims of the Colombian armed conflict.
PAPSIVI attendance data (N = 534,818).

important_claims.claim: Intervention types were broadly provided in line with PAPSIVI guidelines.
important_claims.evidence: Victims experiencing torture, sexual violence, and forced recruitment were more likely to receive individual sessions; those with community-level impacts received community interventions.

important_claims.claim: Those with previous mental health diagnoses had increased odds of attending individual or family sessions but lower odds of group or community sessions.
important_claims.evidence: Regression results consistent with program recommendations for more intensive intervention for higher mental health needs.

important_claims.claim: 29% of individual session attendees only received a single session.
important_claims.evidence: Descriptive statistic reported in results.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a scalable observational framework for evaluating large-scale psychosocial service targeting and engagement using linked administrative data; highlights key equity and retention metrics for replication in other post-conflict settings.

keynotes.title: Dominant negative ATP5F1A variants disrupt oxidative phosphorylation causing neurological disorders

keynote.title: Dominant negative ATP5F1A variants disrupt oxidative phosphorylation causing neurological disorders
keynote.core_problem: Understanding the pathogenic mechanism and clinical spectrum of heterozygous de novo ATP5F1A missense variants associated with neurological disorders.
contributions: Identifies 6 new probands with heterozygous de novo ATP5F1A missense variants presenting with developmental delay, intellectual disability, and movement disorders.
Demonstrates a dominant negative mechanism for all tested variants using C. elegans functional assays.
Shows reduced complex V abundance and activity in patient-derived blood cells and fibroblasts.
Reveals uncoupled oxidative phosphorylation (increased oxygen consumption, decreased mitochondrial membrane potential and ATP) as the pathophysiologic mechanism.
Distinguishes this mechanism clinically and functionally from the previously reported p.Arg207His variant.
Expands the phenotypic and genotypic spectrum of ATP5F1A-associated disorders.
Establishes ATP5F1A as the most frequent nuclear genome cause of complex V deficiency (based on 12 heterozygous individuals reported).
methods: C. elegans functional evaluation to assess variant impact via dominant negative mechanism
Biochemical assays measuring complex V abundance and activity
Proteomics studies
Mitochondrial physiology assays (oxygen consumption, mitochondrial membrane potential, ATP levels) in fibroblasts
experiments_or_evidence: Functional evaluation in C. elegans showing all tested variants are damaging via dominant negative mechanism
Biochemical and proteomics studies showing marked reduction in complex V abundance and activity in proband-derived blood cells and fibroblasts
Mitochondrial physiology studies in fibroblasts showing increased oxygen consumption, decreased mitochondrial membrane potential, and decreased ATP levels

gap_signals.text: Findings contrast functionally and clinically with the previously reported ATP5F1A variant p.Arg207His, suggesting a distinct pathological mechanism.

datasets_or_materials: Proband-derived blood cells
Proband-derived fibroblasts
C. elegans model

important_claims.claim: ATP5F1A variants act via a dominant negative mechanism.
important_claims.evidence: Functional evaluation in C. elegans revealed that all variants tested were damaging to gene function via a dominant negative genetic mechanism.

important_claims.claim: Pathophysiology involves uncoupled oxidative phosphorylation.
important_claims.evidence: Mitochondrial physiology studies in fibroblasts revealed increased oxygen consumption, yet decreased mitochondrial membrane potential and ATP levels.

important_claims.claim: ATP5F1A is the most frequent nuclear genome cause of complex V deficiency.
important_claims.evidence: With 12 heterozygous individuals now reported.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a functional framework (C. elegans assay + biochemical/physiological profiling) for classifying ATP5F1A variants of uncertain significance; highlights uncoupling as a disease mechanism relevant to other OXPHOS disorders.
